chapters.title: Your thought system is a double-edged sword; it can either take you captive or free you, depending on how you wield it
chapters.text:
To be unglued means to be upset, disorganized, and confused when you lose control of your emotions. We all get unglued but to varying degrees. Some of us tend to easily lose control of our emotions, while others seem to be able to control theirs. Frequent or not, we all have those moments when we've had enough and just act out in our unique ways. But it doesn't end there; after acting out, something potentially more devastating begins to happen: we feel guilt and condemnation. No one can save us from this feeling because it's locked up in our minds where only we can access it. That's the reason why it's so dangerous.

The reason mental tortures are so painful is that no one understands what's going on but you.

If you don't learn how to get over guilt and condemnation, you will find yourself repeating the same action over and over again. It sure feels like hell.

Here's why guilt and condemnation reinforce the cycle: at the instance of acting out on your emotions, you could either blame yourself or the other person for what happened. Sometimes you blame yourself, and at other times you blame others — depending on your assessment of the situation. But in any case, you'd leave there feeling bad about yourself. The experience will reinforce the negative thoughts you have about yourself. You'd start reinforcing statements like: “I can never get overacting this way,” “I just have a bad character,” “I knew it, I can't change no matter how I try”.

“I just did it again. God help me, I'm such a terrible mom,” and so on.

The inner dialogue you'll have with yourself will be congruent with the ones you had in your head the last time you acted out. And this reinforces those thoughts, making them a solid part of you. This is why it's so hard to get rid of negative character traits. But there's a way out and that's what this summary is dedicated to showing you!

I can’t control the things that happen to me each day, but I can control how I think about them. ~ Lysa TerKeurst

chapters.title: You are the label you place on yourself
chapters.text:
Do you know why, as an adult, the words others use on you don't matter so much? Because your power of choice has matured over time and you're now able to accept or reject what people say about you.

When someone calls you messy, the words won't have any effect on you if you take them with a pinch of salt knowing the mere fact that your house is messy doesn't mean you're a messy person. But the reason many adults live with broken confidence is that they allowed other people and the circumstances around to place labels on them.

Can you relate?

If your kids are always misbehaving, it's easy to label yourself a terrible mom who can't raise kids properly.

Labeling is a self-imposed trap that far too many adults fall into.

On the contrary, however, you could tell yourself that having kids who misbehave doesn't mean you're a bad mom, it only means you need to find a parenting technique that works better. When you think that way, your mind suddenly changes. You start becoming open to finding parenting techniques that work well. This new mental attitude would help you get out of the situation. But labeling yourself a terrible mom won't help at all. Instead, it shuts you off from seeking ways to better your situation.

Apply the unlabeling principle to every area of your life where you feel stuck and unworthy. Analyze your internal dialogue in that area of your life to determine the negative label you've accepted and refuse it. For example, you can tell yourself, “I am not a disorganized worker. I'm just struggling with being productive and I'd find a way around it.” After affirming the new conviction, go ahead, and seek a solution.

True peacekeeping is about properly processing the emotions before they get stuffed and rot into something horribly toxic. ~ Lysa TerKeurst

Did you know? Naps improve your brain’s day-to-day performance, thereby making you more productive.

chapters.title: You can't control what happens around you, but you have absolute control over your response
chapters.text:
People have been getting on each others' nerves since the dawn of man, and that reality isn't about to change anytime soon. Even couples who have been married for decades still have conflicts, so you shouldn't expect any less from your relationships.

But the good news is, you don't even have to bother about that. All you have to worry about is your response when people wrong you or when circumstances don't go your way.

To live a happy life, take your focus away from things you can't control and focus on the factors you can control.

Let's consider the two types of responses we give when unglued and how we can handle them more appropriately.
• Exploding: When we explode, everybody sees it. This emotional response is different from stuffing — a situation where we keep the hurt to ourselves. Generally, we all tend to be both exploders and stuffers, depending on the situation. Exploding has two types: the situation where you shame yourself and the one where you blame others for your outburst.
• Stuffing: Stuffers generally don't act out at the moment. They do either of two things: take the hurt and push the objects of their hurt away, or they stuff the hurt only to bring it up in a different argument. Ideally, stuffers push people away when they can afford to lose the relationship. This happens a lot with casual friendships. If you've seen people avoid their friends because of something the friend did — that he or she didn't realize — that's stuffing right there. In the case where they can't afford to lose the relationship (family for example) what stuffers do is take a record of the hurt and pour it all out some other day when they've had enough. The person on the receiving end would be surprised at the sudden outburst, not knowing it was a result of emotional stuffing.

So what can you do? How do you ensure you communicate your hurt without aggravating the situation by acting out?

Having a response template is good practice. It may sound bizarre, but it works! Draft out a response template when you're emotionally neutral and can think more clearly. Your response template should have the following elements:
• Start with honoring them, “I understand how you feel, Mary…”
• Clarify your intention and express your hurt: this is especially important if you think there was a misunderstanding
• Apologize if necessary
• Keep it short. This will keep you from being unglued

It's just a template, tweak it as much as you want. The whole purpose is to keep you from being unglued and also to maintain your relationship with the other person.

chapters.title: A grateful heart relates better with people
chapters.text:
You can't control the external world, but you have absolute control over your internal world, and that's where the magic happens. When you learn to take hold of your internal world, you will easily navigate your way around things. A very powerful way to take charge of your internal world is to cultivate the habit of gratitude. It changes everything.

The more grateful you are about your experiences and possessions, the more empathic you become, and the less likely you are to act out on people.

How do you cultivate a heart of gratitude?

Start with praising and worshipping God for all the things he's given you. Remember what the songwriter said? Count your blessing and it would surprise you what the Lord has done. It's so easy to start. All you have to do is incorporate it into your daily time with the Lord. There's no specific formula; you can express your gratitude in the beginning, middle, or at the end of your daily devotion. And if you want to take it a notch higher, start being grateful for things the moment they happen. We tend to do this only when there's a major miracle. But everything is a miracle if you really think about it. So go ahead, start expressing gratitude for the little things in life, and before you know it, you'd start exercising control over your emotions.

chapters.title: Be so busy working on your life that you don't have time to entertain jealousy
chapters.text:
Jealousy and envy are two very common emotions. It's normal to feel jealous when you see someone having the things you have been trying to have, but this feeling becomes destructive when you let it linger for long. It's even worse when you allow it to control your life. For example, a jealous person might begin to feel unworthy of the things they seek in life because someone else has it all and they can't seem to make headway. This feeling is especially hurtful when the person in question is a friend or close relative.

But is there an antidote to jealousy?

You bet.

The antidote lies inside you. If you're able to change the way you approach things in life, you would easily overcome jealousy. Everyone has their lot. And there is time and season for everything. The people you envy have what they have now because it's what they can handle at the moment. The universe conspires to give us only what we're ready to handle.

Picture this: Two friends, Peter and James, go for a music audition. Peter gets a deal while James wasn't chosen, and because of that, James begins to feel jealous. But what he doesn't realize is that Peter had so practiced and refined his talent that he just had to be chosen, James, on the other hand, hasn't put in as much work.

If James could see that he isn't yet qualified, he wouldn't expend any energy being jealous, instead, he'd be happy for his friend and channel his energy into developing himself.

We can’t always fix our circumstances, but we can fix our minds on God. We can do that. ~ Lysa TerKeurst

Easier said than done?

Yes and no.

Yes because at the instance of jealousy, emotions tend to override logic. And no because, by focusing on your own life, you would easily dispel jealousy.

Allowing jealousy to linger more than necessary is like saying, “the other person's life is more important than mine, so I'd focus on theirs and let mine suffer.”

Another strategy to protect yourself from the entanglement of jealousy is cultivating the habit of giving. Use every opportunity you get to show love and kindness to people. This habit will get you genuinely interested in people's progress.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
God gave us emotions so we can connect with the happenings around us. It is by being in touch with our emotions that we're able to develop empathy and extend the love of Christ to the world. How else would you want to relate effectively with a grieving person if you've never lost anything before? How would you be able to show kindness if you don't know how it feels like to be loved?

Do you get the point?

Emotions are important. They aren't bad in themselves but negative emotions exist and how we handle them determines if we get unglued or not.

Sometimes we can't just help reacting negatively to situations and people that make us feel uncomfortable and that's normal. It's okay to act out, but that mustn't become a habit.

If you often lose control and act out, then you undoubtedly already know that you need to find a way out. Nobody likes to live with the guilt and shame that comes with being unglued. But how do you change? Is there a magic wand to wave? Well, not exactly, but there's something that works really well: learn to trust in God and believe him to work on you. We're all imperfect creatures but we can trust in a perfect God to work on our imperfections. Trust in God for a change and take those baby steps toward taming your outbursts. Your progress won't be perfect, but imperfect progress is still progress.

Try this:

Create time to craft a response template to give when someone says or does something that gets on your nerves. Just keep these points in mind: honor them, tell them how they hurt you, and keep it short.

Read it till the end?
